text: Hi, welcome to This is My Architecture.
My name is Andrea and I'm here with Al from Cinefilm.
Hi, Al. Welcome to the show.
Thanks, Andrea.
It's great to be here.
So tell us about Cinefilm.
What do you guys do?
We make software that processes video for retiming,
deinterlacing, and upscaling, and noise removal,
and retexturing.
Great.
So today we're going to talk about PixelStrings.
That is a platform running on AWS.
And I can see a bunch of different services here on the board.
Let's walk through use case.
What is the first thing that happens?
OK.
So PixelStrings is a service that runs on AWS.
And to access the service, a user just
has to use a regular web browser.
We recommend Chrome.
And they log in.
They create an account on the PixelStrings server,
which is running here on EC2 instance.
And so this is a regular internet connection.
And they need to sort of bring their own video clips.
OK.
So I use the internet browser to access your web front end.
Do I then interact by adding content?
Or is that happening?
Yes.
The user uploads their input clips to an S3 bucket that they manage.
OK.
But in the web browser, they need to enter their S3 keys
so that our server, our job manager actually,
can talk to the S3 bucket.
OK.
And then I upload that data.
How do you retrieve that information back to the user
from the UI?
OK.
So we use media and photo running inside Lambda, AWS Lambda
function.
And so the job manager uses Lambda to get metadata from the S3
bucket from each clip inside there.
And so the user initially, if they have an S3 bucket already
set up, and let's say it's got 100 clips in there,
they'll see just only 100 clips with no metadata.
But every second or two, they'll see metadata filled in
in the browser because these calls are going on in the background.
I see.
OK.
So you're capturing the metadata and exposing it.
Is that stored anywhere, or does that happen on the fly?
That is stored.
And it's cached inside a database.
So the job manager, after it gets the metadata,
it saves the metadata to the database.
The web server doesn't talk directly to the job manager.
It actually talks to the database.
And that's how they see the metadata.
I see.
So now I got my data loaded.
I've got the metadata all exposed.
Tell us about what kind of work would you
be able to do as the content uploader?
What is the set of different things
that you can do with the platform now, going from here?
OK.
You can do things like retiming, we call that.
That's our wormhole product.
OK.
And then de-interlacing is taken care of by what
we call spacetime.
And noise removal and retexturing.
That's taken care of by dark energy.
OK.
So these services are exposed.
They're all GPU accelerated.
And the user in the web browser can decide one or more.
So they could have all of them active in their job.
So they define a workflow, including the output codec used
for their output file.
So let's walk through a situation here.
So wormhole, we talked about, or you mentioned,
the customer now want to edit that clip.
And what happens next?
So they choose to do that.
What is the next set of steps that
happens in this ecosystem?
OK.
So they can select one or more clips and one or more workflows
per clip.
They can be different workflows for different clips.
And then they say, go.
And they submit.
And we give them a cost, because we
know how long each clip is from the metadata.
And we give them an estimate.
So we say, this is how much it'll cost.
Do you really want to?
And they say, yes.
They hit submit.
And then the job gets submitted.
And the job manager now, it can send one job to each CPU.
So all these jobs can run in parallel, up to 200 jobs
at once per region.
OK.
Gotcha.
So you have parallel work happening here.
I see G3.
I suspect you were referring to GPU-enabled cluster here.
Is that a fair assumption?
OK.
So the processing happens here.
So how does that then go translate into something
that a customer can now view or have to their disposal?
OK.
So the job manager, after the GPUs
are done processing, they write their output to local disk.
And then the output gets written back to the bucket.
So there's an asset manager here.
So I should have mentioned the job manager tells the asset
manager to copy the input clip to the local disk of the G3.
OK.
That makes sense.
Yeah.
And then it tells when the job, all the GPU processing is done
and encoding is done.
Then job manager tells the asset manager,
copy the output clip back to the customer's bucket.
Yeah.
That makes perfect sense.
You know, in this platform or for this platform,
I can assume many users doing this simultaneously.
Talk to us about scale.
How many users can you accommodate at the same time,
accessing and doing processing?
The web server can handle about 100 users.
We actually haven't pushed it all the way to the limit.
OK.
But it scales pretty well.
And then 200 jobs just because of the number of G3s that we've
been allocated per region.
I see.
So if we're allocated more, we could scale to more jobs.
OK.
And we could always switch the web server to a different EC2
instance to handle more users.
I see.
And does this then also come with multiple instances, or is it
single?
Talk us about high availability.
Right now, it's just a single.
Yeah.
Right now, this is just a single instance.
The high availability, we focused on making our software run on a whole bunch of GPUs at the same time.
OK.
If a GPU fails, and it's very rare because AWS is so reliable, but once in a while,
there'll be some sort of startup glitch.
And in that case, a timeout will occur, the job manager will realize that,
and it will shut down that GPU and start a new one.
OK.
And the job can continue.
OK.
So on an ongoing basis, you have feedback as to what's really happening in your cluster.
And you make sure this is highly available.
I do see IMF.
What does it stand for and what does it do?
OK.
IMF stands for Interoperable Master Format.
It's like the digital cinema package, but it's intended to be like the next step beyond it.
And Netflix encourages their customers to submit clips in the IMF format.
So if anyone wants to publish to Netflix, they need to use IMF.
So how does it work now?
The job execution is being processed by your G3s.
Yes.
Where does IMF come into play?
Is that a next step?
Or does the web server communicate directly with the interoperable master framework?
Yeah.
That's selected by the workflow.
So back in the web browser, if the user wants the output to be IMF in IMF format, then there's
a pull down.
They can say, all right, do this.
And they have to enter IMF metadata.
That gets sent.
The job manager knows it's IMF.
So when the GPU processing is done and the encoding is done, the job manager says, oh, by the way,
this is an IMF job.
It doesn't tell the asset manager to copy that output.
Instead, it sends the data to IMF.
Okay.
IMF wrapping takes place.
And then the asset manager copies the result.
Oh, copies back to the user.
Yes.
Okay.
That makes perfectly sense.
So let's talk about geographic reach.
Okay.
Is this accessible for users across the world?
What is the geographic reach here?
We've got GPU clusters in US West, US East, and Ireland.
And so users with buckets in those regions, they don't have to pay huge egress costs.
Okay.
Because our EC2 instances are close to their bucket.
I see.
It's an exciting opportunity here.
Where do you see this go?
What is your vision for the future?
In the future, we're going to add more codecs, better retiming, including better audio retiming,
more scalability.
We do want to make the web server have a real-time fallover to another server.
Yeah.
That's...
Great.
So thank you, Al, for walking us through this architecture.
You talked about essentially a user interface for processing data on GPUs and then providing
the output files back to the user on S3.
Yes.
Thank you very much for being here.
Thank you.
Thank you very much for watching.
This is my architecture.
This is my architecture.
I'm an electrical user interface.
I said to review timekeeping software that I've taken, unfortunately.
